title: Responsible Lending Notification (SorKorChor. 7/2566)
summary: BOT's core responsible lending framework requiring ability-to-repay assessments, DSR limits, and Risk-Based Pricing requirements. Updated Jan 2025.
compliance_notes: Primary Thai regulatory anchor for any credit scoring system. Non-compliance = supervisory sanctions.
citation: Bank of Thailand. (2023, updated 2025). Responsible Lending Notification (SorKorChor. 7/2566). Bank of Thailand.

title: Debt Service Ratio (DSR) Macroprudential Policy
summary: BOT macroprudential policy setting maximum DSR thresholds by loan type to prevent household over-leveraging. Core hard constraint for credit underwriting models.
compliance_notes: DSR computation must be embedded as a hard constraint in the credit scoring pipeline, not just a feature.
citation: Bank of Thailand. (2023). Debt Service Ratio Macroprudential Policy. Bank of Thailand.

title: Risk-Based Pricing (RBP) Regulatory Sandbox
summary: BOT sandbox allowing rate differentiation based on individual credit risk scores. Requires model transparency and fairness demonstration.
compliance_notes: AI credit scoring feeding pricing decisions may be subject to RBP sandbox requirements.
citation: Bank of Thailand. (2022, updated 2024). Risk-Based Pricing Regulatory Sandbox. Bank of Thailand.

title: National Credit Bureau (NCB) Thailand — Credit Score Overview
summary: Thailand's official credit bureau. Score range 300–900 based on payment history, utilization, history length, mix, inquiries. Thin-file problem: many Thais lack NCB history.
compliance_notes: NCB data use must comply with PDPA and NCB member data-sharing agreements.
citation: National Credit Bureau. (2024). NCB Credit Score Overview. National Credit Bureau of Thailand.

title: Personal Data Protection Act B.E. 2562 (PDPA) — Credit Scoring Implications
organization: Office of the Personal Data Protection Committee (PDPC)
summary: Thailand's primary data protection law. Credit scoring models must have lawful basis for processing, allow data subject rights, and permit human review of automated decisions.
compliance_notes: All Thai-market credit AI must document PDPA lawful basis. Automated rejection must allow human review.
citation: Office of the PDPC. (2022). Personal Data Protection Act B.E. 2562. Royal Gazette.

title: SME Credit Risk Database and NaCGA Credit Guarantee Scheme
summary: BOT SME credit risk database and NaCGA partial guarantees enabling credit access for SMEs lacking collateral. Relevant for SME-specific scoring model design.
compliance_notes: SME models must account for NaCGA guarantee interactions and BOT SME reporting requirements.
citation: Bank of Thailand. (2024). SME Credit Risk Database. Bank of Thailand.

title: ADB Working Paper: Access to Finance for SMEs in Thailand
summary: ADB analysis of Thai SME financing barriers. Cash flow-based lending outperforms collateral-based. Alternative data (tax, utility, e-commerce) can bridge gaps.
citation: Asian Development Bank. (2021). Access to Finance for SMEs in Thailand. ADB Working Paper.

title: ASEAN SME Financing Benchmark and Digital Lending Landscape
summary: ASEAN benchmark on SME financing and digital lending. Thailand SME credit ~40% GDP. Invoice/supply chain finance most promising. Data sharing between tax/banks would unlock scoring improvements.
citation: ASEAN Secretariat / ADB. (2022). ASEAN SME Financing Benchmark. ADB.

title: Bank of Thailand FinTech Regulatory Sandbox — Digital Lending
summary: BOT sandbox for FinTech digital lending. Allows testing of AI credit scoring under regulatory supervision. Alternative data (telecom, e-wallet) permitted under sandbox.
compliance_notes: New AI credit scoring systems in Thailand may benefit from sandbox participation.
citation: Bank of Thailand. (2024). FinTech Regulatory Sandbox — Digital Lending. Bank of Thailand.

title: Thai Financial Reporting Standard 9 (TFRS 9) — Expected Credit Loss (ECL)
summary: Thai IFRS 9 adoption requiring forward-looking ECL provisioning using PD×LGD×EAD models. Three-stage model. Macro-economic overlays required. Effective 2020.
compliance_notes: Thai banks must maintain TFRS 9 ECL models. Credit scoring PD feeds ECL. Model alignment critical.
use_case_tags: tfrs9; ecl; pd_model; regulatory_compliance; thai_market; ifrs9
citation: Federation of Accounting Professions. (2020). Thai Financial Reporting Standard 9. FAP Thailand.

title: FICO Score — Understanding Your Credit Score (5 Factor Model)
summary: FICO 5-factor model: Payment History 35%, Amounts Owed 30%, Credit History 15%, Credit Mix 10%, New Credit 10%. Score range 300–850. Global benchmark for consumer credit scoring.
compliance_notes: FICO factors are baseline for feature engineering. Direct FICO score use in Thailand requires licensing.
citation: Fair Isaac Corporation. (2024). What's in Your Credit Score? myFICO.

title: Weight of Evidence (WoE) and Information Value (IV) for Logistic Regression Scorecards
summary: Comprehensive WoE/IV methodology guide. WoE = ln(Events%/NonEvents%). IV thresholds for variable selection. Scorecard points conversion. Standard for Basel IRB PD models.
compliance_notes: WoE/LR scorecards are most regulatory-accepted. Preferred for Basel IRB PD model documentation.
citation: Bhalla, D. (2023). Weight of Evidence and Information Value. listendata.com.

title: Credit Risk Scorecard Development Using WoE and Logistic Regression
summary: End-to-end scorecard development pipeline: data prep, binning, WoE, logistic regression, Gini/KS/AUC validation, PSI monitoring, scorecard calibration.
citation: Altair. (2022). Credit Risk Scorecard Development. Altair.

title: Logistic Regression and WoE Scorecard: Credit Risk Modeling Best Practices
summary: Academic formalization of WoE/IV methodology. Covers theoretical basis, OOT validation, CSI/PSI stability. Preferred validation: out-of-time (OOT) sample over K-fold.
compliance_notes: WoE/LR scorecard is gold standard for Basel IRB PD models. Validators and auditors familiar with this.
citation: Author et al. (2018). Logistic Regression Credit Risk Scorecard. Quantitative Finance, Taylor & Francis.

title: Machine Learning Methods for Credit Risk: Comparative Study
summary: Comparative study of XGBoost, LightGBM, Random Forest vs logistic regression on AUC for credit default. Ensemble models outperform. Model stacking offers marginal gains.
compliance_notes: ML models must be paired with SHAP/LIME for regulatory acceptance. Pure black-box not acceptable.
citation: Author et al. (2023). Machine Learning Methods for Credit Risk. PLOS ONE.

title: Deep Learning and Ensemble Methods for Credit Scoring: Systematic Review
summary: 2024 systematic review of LSTM, Transformer, GNN approaches for credit scoring. Tabular data: tree-based ensembles still competitive. SMOTE/ADASYN for class imbalance.
compliance_notes: Deep learning models face highest regulatory scrutiny; require robust XAI layer.
citation: Author et al. (2024). Deep Learning for Credit Scoring. Nature Scientific Reports.

title: A Comparative Study of ML Algorithms for Credit Default Prediction
summary: CatBoost competitive for categorical data. SMOTE+undersampling best for imbalance. AUC-PR more informative than AUC-ROC for imbalanced data. Model calibration critical for PD.
compliance_notes: Model calibration required for PD estimation under Basel IRB.
citation: Author et al. (2023). Comparative Study of ML for Credit Default. arXiv preprint.

title: SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique)
summary: Seminal SMOTE paper for handling class imbalance in binary classification. Generates synthetic minority (default) samples by interpolation. Combine with undersampling for best results.
compliance_notes: Document imbalance handling approach in model documentation for regulatory review.
citation: Chawla, N. V. et al. (2002). SMOTE: Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique. JAIR.

title: Alternative Data for Credit Risk Assessment
summary: World Bank report on telecom, utility, e-commerce, psychometric, social media data for thin-file credit scoring. Most predictive: telecom, utility, e-commerce. Social media has higher bias risk.
compliance_notes: Alternative data in Thailand must comply with PDPA. Consent required. Telecom needs carrier agreements.
citation: World Bank / CGAP. (2022). Alternative Data for Credit Risk Assessment. World Bank.

title: Big Data and Machine Learning for Credit Access and Financial Inclusion
summary: FinTech lenders with alternative data approve 30–40% more borrowers at same default rates. Digital footprint predictive but raises privacy concerns. Disparate impact risk.
compliance_notes: Disparate impact analysis essential when using digital footprint or behavioral data.
citation: Author et al. (2020). Big Data and ML for Financial Inclusion. SSRN Working Paper.

title: AFI Report: Digital Financial Services and Alternative Credit Scoring
summary: Mobile money transaction history strongly predictive. Agent banking data substitute for rural areas. Thailand, Philippines, Indonesia highlighted as active markets.
compliance_notes: Relevant for Thai rural/low-income segments. Mobile wallet (PromptPay, TrueMoney) as alternative data.
citation: Alliance for Financial Inclusion. (2020). Digital Financial Services and Alternative Credit Scoring. AFI.

title: SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) — Original Paper and Documentation
summary: Leading XAI method based on game theory Shapley values. Works with any model (Tree SHAP exact/fast, Kernel SHAP model-agnostic). Produces force plots, summary plots. Enables adverse action reason codes.
compliance_notes: SHAP recommended for credit scoring adverse action explanations. Satisfies ECOA/Regulation B and EU AI Act.
use_case_tags: xai; shap; explainability; adverse_action; feature_attribution; regulatory_compliance
citation: Lundberg, S. M., & Lee, S. I. (2017). A Unified Approach to Interpreting Model Predictions. NeurIPS.

title: LIME (Local Interpretable Model-agnostic Explanations)
summary: LIME generates local surrogate linear models for individual predictions. Model-agnostic. Less stable than SHAP. Useful as secondary validation / comparison method.
compliance_notes: LIME acceptable but SHAP preferred for consistency. Use as secondary validation tool.
citation: Ribeiro, M. T. et al. (2016). 'Why Should I Trust You?': Explaining Any Classifier. KDD 2016.

title: Explainable AI in Credit Scoring: Regulatory Requirements and Implementation
summary: CFA Institute report on XAI requirements in financial services. Minimum: top 3–5 reason codes per decision. Model governance: version control, validation, monitoring, human-in-the-loop.
compliance_notes: Relevant for BOT credit model governance requirements and EU AI Act compliance.
citation: CFA Institute. (2023). Explainable AI in Finance. CFA Institute Research.

title: Explainability in Credit Risk Models: Hybrid SHAP-Scorecard Approaches
summary: Hybrid architecture: XGBoost base + SHAP reason code layer. 5–8% AUC gain over pure scorecard. SHAP reason codes mapped to human-readable adverse action statements. Explanation audit trail.
compliance_notes: Hybrid SHAP-scorecard is recommended architecture for Thai credit AI deployment.
citation: Author et al. (2023). Explainability in Credit Risk: Hybrid Approaches. Applied Intelligence, Springer.

title: CFPB Guidance on AI/ML in Credit Decisions — Adverse Action Notices
summary: CFPB: ECOA adverse action requirements apply equally to complex AI/ML models. Specific accurate reason codes required. 'The model decided' is NOT acceptable.
compliance_notes: While US-specific, BOT has analogous requirements. Any Thai credit AI should adopt this as best practice.
citation: Consumer Financial Protection Bureau. (2022). CFPB Acts to Protect from Illegal Credit Denials. CFPB.

title: CFPB Fair Lending Annual Report 2023 — AI/ML Examination Focus
summary: CFPB 2023 fair lending focus on AI/ML bias. LDA analysis now required. Disparate impact: proxy variables for protected class trigger scrutiny. Enforcement actions brought.
compliance_notes: LDA analysis is becoming global best practice. Thai credit AI should document LDA testing.
citation: Consumer Financial Protection Bureau. (2024). Fair Lending Report of the CFPB 2023. CFPB.

title: EU AI Act — Annex III High-Risk AI: Credit Scoring Classification
summary: Credit scoring explicitly listed as High-Risk AI (Annex III 5b). Requirements: conformity assessment, technical documentation, transparency, human oversight, fundamental rights impact assessment. Effective Aug 2026.
compliance_notes: Thai institutions with EU customers/investors must plan for Aug 2026. Sets global gold standard.
citation: European Parliament. (2024). EU AI Act Annex III. Official Journal of the European Union.

title: Fairness in Machine Learning — Survey and Taxonomy
summary: Comprehensive JMLR survey of ML fairness definitions. Demographic parity, equalized odds, individual fairness, counterfactual fairness. Impossibility theorem. Pre/in/post-processing mitigation.
compliance_notes: Understanding fairness taxonomy essential for ECOA/EU AI Act non-discrimination documentation.
citation: Mehrabi, N. et al. (2019). A Survey on Bias and Fairness in Machine Learning. JMLR / arXiv.

title: Basel III Internal Ratings-Based (IRB) Approach — Credit Risk
summary: BIS Basel framework. PD (Probability of Default), LGD (Loss Given Default), EAD (Exposure at Default). Expected Loss = PD×LGD×EAD. Foundation IRB vs Advanced IRB. Model validation requirements.
compliance_notes: BOT-supervised banks using IRB must comply with BOT's Basel implementation. PD = credit scoring model.
citation: Basel Committee on Banking Supervision. (2006, updated 2023). Basel III. BIS.

title: Probability of Default Model Development: Practical Guide
summary: BoE PRA guidance on TTC vs PIT PD estimation, model uncertainty margin of conservatism, documentation standards, backtesting requirements.
compliance_notes: BOT Thai banks follow analogous requirements. PIT PD required for TFRS 9 ECL provisioning.
citation: Bank of England PRA. (2023). Probability of Default Models. Bank of England.

title: World Bank Global Findex Database 2021
summary: 148-country financial inclusion database. Thailand: ~95% bank account ownership. Formal credit access lower. Mobile money growing. Gender gap in credit persists.
citation: World Bank. (2022). The Global Findex Database 2021. World Bank Group.

title: IFC Credit Infrastructure and Responsible Finance — SME Credit Access
summary: IFC framework for credit infrastructure (bureau, collateral registry, insolvency). SME credit gap globally = USD 5.2 trillion. Credit bureau coverage key to reducing information asymmetry.
citation: International Finance Corporation. (2023). Credit Infrastructure. IFC World Bank Group.

title: Population Stability Index (PSI) and Model Monitoring Best Practices
summary: PSI for detecting model input drift and output distribution shifts. PSI<0.1 stable; 0.1–0.2 investigate; >0.2 rebuild. CSI per variable. Monthly PSI, quarterly CSI, annual validation.
compliance_notes: PSI monitoring required under BOT model risk guidelines and Basel IRB use test.
citation: Bhalla, D. (2023). Population Stability Index. listendata.com.

title: Model Risk Management — SR 11-7 (OCC/Federal Reserve)
summary: SR 11-7 defines model risk and three lines of defense. Validation: conceptual soundness, data quality, outcomes analysis, benchmarking. Model inventory required. Challenger models.
compliance_notes: BOT model risk guidelines largely aligned with SR 11-7. All credit models must follow this framework.
citation: Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System. (2011). SR 11-7: Model Risk Management. Federal Reserve.

title: RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) — Original Paper
summary: Original RAG paper: dense retrieval + seq2seq generation. Reduces hallucination by grounding in retrieved facts. Chunk size and retrieval quality impact answer quality. Hybrid BM25+embedding recommended.
citation: Lewis, P. et al. (2020). Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks. NeurIPS.
